#!/bin/bash

# Load system cryptographic policy
CRYPTO_POL_FILE="/etc/crypto-policies/backends/system-policy"

# Check if the crypto policy file exists
if [ ! -f "$CRYPTO_POL_FILE" ]; then
    echo "System cryptographic policy file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read the FIPS compliant cipher string from the system policy
FIPS_CIPHERS=$(grep 'Ciphers' "$CRYPTO_POL_FILE" | awk '{print $3}')

# Check if the FIPS ciphers are uncommented and present in the SSH config
SSH_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
if grep -q "Ciphers.*$FIPS_CIPHERS" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
    echo "SSH server is already configured with FIPS compliant ciphers."
else
    # Backup current SSH configuration
    cp "$SSH_CONFIG" "$SSH_CONFIG.bak"

    # Update SSH config with FIPS compliant ciphers
    sed -i "/Ciphers/s/^.*$/Ciphers $FIPS_CIPHERS/" "$SSH_CONFIG"
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "SSH server configured with FIPS compliant ciphers."
    else
        echo "Failed to update SSH configuration."
        # Restore original SSH config if sed failed
        cp "$SSH_CONFIG.bak" "$SSH_CONFIG"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Reload SSH service to apply changes
systemctl reload sshd
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure only FIPS 140-2 validated MACs are used for OpenSSH server
SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
CRYPTO_POLICIES=$(openssl version -v | grep 'built on' | awk '{print $NF}' | cut -d. -f1,2)

# Check if the system is FIPS compliant
if [[ "$CRYPTO_POLICIES" =~ ^(1\.0|1\.1) ]]; then
    # Define a list of FIPS 140-2 validated MAC algorithms
    FIPS_MACS="hmac-sha1-etm@ssh hmac-sha256-etm@ssh"

    # Remove any existing local overrides for MACs in sshd_config
    sed -i '/^MAC/s/^.*$/#&/' $SSHD_CONFIG

    # Add the FIPS 140-2 validated MAC algorithms to sshd_config
    echo "MAC $FIPS_MACS" >> $SSHD_CONFIG
else
    echo "System is not built with FIPS 140-2 cryptographic policies."
fi

# Reload SSH daemon configuration
systemctl reload sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Set GNOME screensaver lock-delay to 0 minutes for all users
gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-delay 0

# Ensure that the setting is applied by reloading the session configuration
dbus-send --session --dest=org.gnome.SessionManager \
           --type=method_call /org/gnome/SessionManager \
           org.gnome.SessionManager.RequestRestart string:''

# Set policy to prevent users from changing the lock-delay setting
POLICY_FILE="/etc/dconf/db/local.d/00-screensaver-lock-delay.conf"
if [ ! -f "$POLICY_FILE" ]; then
    echo "create_dir /org/gnome/desktop/session" > "$POLICY_FILE"
fi

echo "key /org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay string \"0\"" >> "$POLICY_FILE"

# Apply the policy changes
dconf update

# Define the default session idle delay in seconds
SESSION_IDLE_DELAY=300

# Create a dconf schema file if it does not exist
if [ ! -f /usr/share/gnome-session/schemas/session.idle.gschema.xml ]; then
    cat <<EOF > /usr/share/gnome-session/schemas/session.idle.gschema.xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<schema>
  <key>/org/gnome/session/idle-delay</key>
  <type>int</type>
  <default>${SESSION_IDLE_DELAY}</default>
</schema>
EOF
fi

# Install dconf schema if it is not already installed
if ! dconf schema-list | grep -q '/org/gnome/session'; then
    dconf load /usr/share/gnome-session/schemas/session.idle.gschema.xml
fi

# Set the session idle delay to the default value
dconf write /org/gnome/session/idle-delay ${SESSION_IDLE_DELAY}

# Ensure that changes persist across reboots and dconf database updates
systemctl restart gdm

#!/bin/bash

# Check if Ctrl-Alt-Del is already disabled
if grep -qE '^xdg-screensaver lock' /etc/xdg/screensaver.conf; then
    echo "Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence is already disabled."
else
    # Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del
    sed -i '/^xdg-screensaver/a xdg-screensaver lock' /etc/xdg/screensaver.conf

    # Lock the file to prevent users from overriding it
    chattr +i /etc/xdg/screensaver.conf

    echo "Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence has been disabled and locked."
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Check if Ctrl-Alt-Del is already disabled
if grep -qE '^xdg-screensaver lock' /etc/xdg/screensaver.conf; then
    echo "Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence is already disabled."
else
    # Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del
    sed -i '/^xdg-screensaver/a xdg-screensaver lock' /etc/xdg/screensaver.conf

    # Lock the file to prevent users from overriding it
    chattr +i /etc/xdg/screensaver.conf

    echo "Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence has been disabled and locked."
fi
```

#!/bin/bash

# Set the sudoers file location
SUDOERS_FILE="/etc/sudoers"

# Check if the current user is root to ensure proper permissions for editing sudoers
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root."
    exit 1
fi

# Define the timeout value in seconds (e.g., 5 minutes = 300 seconds)
CREDENTIAL_CACHE_TIMEOUT=300

# Use visudo to edit the sudoers file safely and ensure idempotency
visudo -c '/NOPASSWD: ALL' || true
sed -i "/Defaults\s\+env_reset/s/^/#/" "$SUDOERS_FILE" || true
sed -i "/ Defaults\s\+timestamp_timeout=/s/^#*//g" "$SUDOERS_FILE" || true
sed -i "/ Defaults\s\+timestamp_timeout/ s/=.*/= ${CREDENTIAL_CACHE_TIMEOUT}/g" "$SUDOERS_FILE" || true

# Check if the timestamp_timeout line exists after editing
if grep -q "Defaults\ttimestamp_timeout" "$SUDOERS_FILE"; then
    echo "sudo credential caching timeout is set to $CREDENTIAL_CACHE_TIMEOUT seconds."
else
    echo "Failed to set sudo credential caching timeout."
    exit 1
fi

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Set the sudoers file location
SUDOERS_FILE="/etc/sudoers"

# Check if the current user is root to ensure proper permissions for editing sudoers
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root."
    exit 1
fi

# Define the timeout value in seconds (e.g., 5 minutes = 300 seconds)
CREDENTIAL_CACHE_TIMEOUT=300

# Use visudo to edit the sudoers file safely and ensure idempotency
visudo -c '/NOPASSWD: ALL' || true
sed -i "/Defaults\s\+env_reset/s/^/#/" "$SUDOERS_FILE" || true
sed -i "/ Defaults\s\+timestamp_timeout=/s/^#*//g" "$SUDOERS_FILE" || true
sed -i "/ Defaults\s\+timestamp_timeout/ s/=.*/= ${CREDENTIAL_CACHE_TIMEOUT}/g" "$SUDOERS_FILE" || true

# Check if the timestamp_timeout line exists after editing
if grep -q "Defaults\ttimestamp_timeout" "$SUDOERS_FILE"; then
    echo "sudo credential caching timeout is set to $CREDENTIAL_CACHE_TIMEOUT seconds."
else
    echo "Failed to set sudo credential caching timeout."
    exit 1
fi
```

#!/bin/bash

if ! command -v gpg &> /dev/null; then

#!/bin/bash

# Check if profile-based authentication tool is installed
if ! command -v authconfig &> /dev/null; then
    echo "authconfig is not installed. Installing now..."
    dnf install -y authconfig
fi

# Select and activate a supported authentication profile
echo "Selecting and activating a supported authentication profile..."

# Set the authentication method to 'systemd' for RHEL 8
authconfig --updateall --enablemd5 --enableshadow --passminlen=8 --enablepam --useshadow --kickstart

# Ensure changes persist across reboots by updating the system configuration files
echo "Updating system configuration files to ensure persistence..."

# Update /etc/nsswitch.conf if necessary
if grep -q 'passwd: files' /etc/nsswitch.conf; then
    sed -i '/^passwd:/ s/files/pam/' /etc/nsswitch.conf
else
    echo 'passwd: files pam' >> /etc/nsswitch.conf
fi

# Update /etc/login.defs if necessary
if grep -q 'ENCRYPT_METHOD SHA512' /etc/login.defs; then
    sed -i '/^ENCRYPT_METHOD/ s/^.*$/ENCRYPT_METHOD SHA512/' /etc/login.defs
else
    echo 'ENCRYPT_METHOD SHA512' >> /etc/login.defs
fi

# Update the authentication configuration to use the new settings
authconfig --updateall

echo "User authentication is now configured through a profile-based tool."

#!/bin/bash

# Check if profile-based authentication tool is installed
if ! command -v authconfig &> /dev/null; then
    echo "authconfig is not installed. Installing now..."
    dnf install -y authconfig
fi

# Select and activate a supported authentication profile
echo "Selecting and activating a supported authentication profile..."

# Set the authentication method to 'systemd' for RHEL 8
authconfig --updateall --enablemd5 --enableshadow --passminlen=8 --enablepam --useshadow --kickstart

# Ensure changes persist across reboots by updating the system configuration files
echo "Updating system configuration files to ensure persistence..."

# Update /etc/nsswitch.conf if necessary
if grep -q 'passwd: files' /etc/nsswitch.conf; then
    sed -i '/^passwd:/ s/files/pam/' /etc/nsswitch.conf
else
    echo 'passwd: files pam' >> /etc/nsswitch.conf
fi

# Update /etc/login.defs if necessary
if grep -q 'ENCRYPT_METHOD SHA512' /etc/login.defs; then
    sed -i '/^ENCRYPT_METHOD/ s/^.*$/ENCRYPT_METHOD SHA512/' /etc/login.defs
else
    echo 'ENCRYPT_METHOD SHA512' >> /etc/login.defs
fi

# Update the authentication configuration to use the new settings
authconfig --updateall

echo "User authentication is now configured through a profile-based tool."

#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to check password quality
check_password_quality() {
    local password="$1"
    local length=${#password}
    local upper=0 lower=0 digit=0 symbol=0
    for (( i=0; i<length; i++ )); do
        char="${password:i:1}"
        if [[ "$char" =~ [A-Z] ]]; then
            ((upper++))
        elif [[ "$char" =~ [a-z] ]]; then
            ((lower++))
        elif [[ "$char" =~ [0-9] ]]; then
            ((digit++))
        else
            ((symbol++))
        fi

        # Check for 5 or more consecutive characters from the same class
        if [[ $upper -ge 5 || $lower -ge 5 || $digit -ge 5 || $symbol -ge 5 ]]; then
            echo "Password rejected: contains too many consecutive characters from the same class."
            return 1
        fi

        # Reset counters for next character
        upper=0 lower=0 digit=0 symbol=0
    done
    return 0
}

# Read current password policy settings
current_policy=$(grep 'password_quality' /etc/login.defs)

# Check if the policy is already set as required
if [[ "$current_policy" =~ ^\s*password_quality\s*yes ]]; then
    echo "Password quality policy is already enabled."
else
    # Enable password quality policy
    sed -i '/^password_quality/a password_quality yes' /etc/login.defs
fi

# Apply the new password quality check to the 'chpasswd' command
sed -i '/^chpasswd/s/^/#/' /etc/pam.d/chpasswd
cat <<EOF >>/etc/pam.d/chpasswd
auth required pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 enforce_for_root yes retry=3
account requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 enforce_for_root yes retry=3

EOF

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to check password quality
check_password_quality() {
    local password="$1"
    local length=${#password}
    local upper=0 lower=0 digit=0 symbol=0
    for (( i=0; i<length; i++ )); do
        char="${password:i:1}"
        if [[ "$char" =~ [A-Z] ]]; then
            ((upper++))
        elif [[ "$char" =~ [a-z] ]]; then
            ((lower++))
        elif [[ "$char" =~ [0-9] ]]; then
            ((digit++))
        else
            ((symbol++))
        fi

        # Check for 5 or more consecutive characters from the same class
        if [[ $upper -ge 5 || $lower -ge 5 || $digit -ge 5 || $symbol -ge 5 ]]; then
            echo "Password rejected: contains too many consecutive characters from the same class."
            return 1
        fi

        # Reset counters for next character
        upper=0 lower=0 digit=0 symbol=0
    done
    return 0
}

# Read current password policy settings
current_policy=$(grep 'password_quality' /etc/login.defs)

# Check if the policy is already set as required
if [[ "$current_policy" =~ ^\s*password_quality\s*yes ]]; then
    echo "Password quality policy is already enabled."
else
    # Enable password quality policy
    sed -i '/^password_quality/a password_quality yes' /etc/login.defs
fi

# Apply the new password quality check to the 'chpasswd' command
sed -i '/^chpasswd/s/^/#/' /etc/pam.d/chpasswd
cat <<EOF >>/etc/pam.d/chpasswd
auth required pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 enforce_for_root yes retry=3
account requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 enforce_for_root yes retry=3
password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1 enforce_for_root yes retry=3
EOF

# Create a new user to test the password policy
new_user="testuser"
useradd "$new_user"

# Set a password for the new user and check if it passes the quality check
echo "${new_user}:newpassword" | chpasswd
if check_password_quality "newpassword"; then
    echo "Password accepted: meets the quality requirements."
else
    echo "Password rejected: does not meet the quality requirements."
fi

# Remove the test user after testing
userdel "$new_user"
```

#!/bin/bash

# Check if /etc/inittab exists (RHEL 8 uses systemd)
if [ -f /etc/inittab ]; then
    # Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot in /etc/inittab
    sed -i '/ca::ctrlaltdel:/s/^/#/' /etc/inittab
else
    # Check if sysctl is available to modify kernel parameters
    if command -v sysctl &> /dev/null; then
        # Set the kernel parameter 'reboot' to 0 to disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot
        sysctl -w kernel.reboot=0 > /dev/null
        # Make this change persistent across reboots by adding it to /etc/sysctl.conf
        echo "kernel.reboot = 0" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
    fi
fi

# Ensure the changes take effect immediately
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    systemctl daemon-reload > /dev/null
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Check if /etc/inittab exists (RHEL 8 uses systemd)
if [ -f /etc/inittab ]; then
    # Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot in /etc/inittab
    sed -i '/ca::ctrlaltdel:/s/^/#/' /etc/inittab
else
    # Check if sysctl is available to modify kernel parameters
    if command -v sysctl &> /dev/null; then
        # Set the kernel parameter 'reboot' to 0 to disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot
        sysctl -w kernel.reboot=0 > /dev/null
        # Make this change persistent across reboots by adding it to /etc/sysctl.conf
        echo "kernel.reboot = 0" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
    fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define the idle time limit in seconds (e.g., 300 seconds = 5 minutes)
IDLE_TIME_LIMIT=300

# Path to the login manager configuration file
CONFIG_FILE="/etc/systemd/system/login.defs"

# Check if the configuration file exists, if not create it with default values
if [ ! -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Creating login.defs with default settings..."
    cat <<EOF > "$CONFIG_FILE"

CONSOLE_TIMEOUT=900
IDLE_TIME=600
EOF
fi

# Update the idle time limit in the configuration file
sed -i "/^IDLE_TIME=/c\IDLE_TIME=$IDLE_TIME_LIMIT" "$CONFIG_FILE"

# Reload systemd manager configuration to apply changes
systemctl daemon-reload

# Restart login manager to apply the new settings
systemctl restart gdm || systemctl restart sddm || systemctl restart lightdm

#!/bin/bash

# Set maximum password age to 365 days
max_age=365

# Loop through all existing local user accounts
while IFS= read -r user; do
    # Check if the account has a different max password age set
    current_max_age=$(chage -l "$user" | grep 'Maximum' | awk '{print $4}')
    
    # If the current max password age is not 365, change it
    if [ "$current_max_age" != "$max_age" ]; then
        chage -M "$max_age" "$user"
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

#!/bin/bash

# Fetch all user accounts from /etc/shadow file
users=$(cut -d: -f1 /etc/shadow)

for user in $users; do
    # Get last password change time and current time
    last_change=$(chage -l $user | grep "Last Password Change" | awk '{print $3}')
    current_time=$(date +%s)
    
    # Convert last change to seconds since epoch
    last_change_seconds=$(date --date="$last_change" +%s)
    
    # Calculate the difference in days
    diff_days=$(( (current_time - last_change_seconds) / 86400 ))
    
    # If the password was changed less than 1 day ago, enforce minimum age
    if [ $diff_days -lt 1 ]; then
        # Set minimum password age to 1 day
        chage -d $(date --date="1 day ago" +%s) $user
    fi
done

    diff_days=$(( (current_time - last_change_seconds) / 86400 ))
    
    # If the password was changed less than 1 day ago, enforce minimum age
    if [ $diff_days -lt 1 ]; then
        # Set minimum password age to 1 day
        chage -d $(date --date="1 day ago" +%s) $user
    fi
done
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define an array of common groups for interactive users
common_groups=("users" "wheel")

# Loop through each user in /etc/passwd
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Extract username and home directory from the line
    username=$(echo "$line" | cut -d: -f1)
    homedir=$(echo "$line" | cut -d: -f6)

    # Skip if the user is not interactive or does not have a home directory
    if [[ ! "$homedir" =~ ^/ && "$username" != "root" ]]; then
        # Loop through each common group
        for group in "${common_groups[@]}"; do
            # Check if the user is a member of the group
            if groups "$username" | grep -q "^$group:"; then
                # Set the group ownership recursively for all files and directories in the home directory
                chgrp -R "$group" "$homedir"
            fi
        done
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

#!/bin/bash

# Function to set correct permissions for dot files
set_permissions() {
    local user=$1
    local homedir=$2
    find "$homedir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name '.*' | while read -r file; do
        local current_perm=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
        if [ "$current_perm" -gt "0740" ]; then
            chmod 0740 "$file"
        fi
    done
}

# Get list of all users including root
users=$(getent passwd | cut -d: -f1)

# Loop through each user and set correct permissions for their dot files
for user in $users; do
    if [ "$user" != "root" ]; then
        homedir=$(eval echo ~$user)
    else
        homedir="/root"
    fi

    # Check if the home directory exists
    if [ -d "$homedir" ]; then
        set_permissions "$user" "$homedir"
    fi
done

#!/bin/bash

# Function to set correct permissions for dot files
set_permissions() {
    local user=$1
    local homedir=$2
    find "$homedir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name '.*' | while read -r file; do
        local current_perm=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
        if [ "$current_perm" -gt "0740" ]; then
            chmod 0740 "$file"
        fi
    done
}

# Get list of all users including root
users=$(getent passwd | cut -d: -f1)

# Loop through each user and set correct permissions for their dot files
for user in $users; do
    if [ "$user" != "root" ]; then
        homedir=$(eval echo ~$user)
    else
        homedir="/root"
    fi

    # Check if the home directory exists
    if [ -d "$homedir" ]; then
        set_permissions "$user" "$homedir"
    fi
done

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure required packages are installed for TLS encryption
if ! rpm -q audit-libs-python3; then
    yum install -y audit-libs-python3
fi

# Configure rsyslog to use encrypted transport
cat <<EOF | tee /etc/rsyslog.d/01-encrypted-forward.conf
$ModLoad imtcp
$InputTCPServerRun 0
$ModLoad imuxsock
$InputUnixSocket /dev/log
$ModLoad imjournal
$InputJournalSyslogFacilityLocal0

# Forward to remote collector using TLS
$RemoteHost <remote_collector_ip>
$RemotePort 514 # Default port for syslog over TCP
$ActionQueueType Linked
$ActionQueueDepth 4
$ActionResumeRetryCount -1

# Enable TLS encryption
$IMTPStreamDriver "ssl"
$IMTPStreamDriverOptions "cert=/etc/ssl/certs/rsyslog.crt key=/etc/ssl/private/rsyslog.key verify=peer"

# Define the template for encrypted messages
$template EncryptedSyslog "%msg%"
EOF

# Generate self-signed certificate and private key if not present
if [ ! -f /etc/ssl/certs/rsyslog.crt ] || [ ! -f /etc/ssl/private/rsyslog.key ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/ssl/private
    openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
        -keyout /etc/ssl/private/rsyslog.key -out /etc/ssl/certs/rsyslog.crt \
        -subj "/C=US/ST=State/L=City/O=Organization/CN=rsyslog"
fi

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Ensure rsyslog is enabled on boot
systemctl enable rsyslog

# Ensure netfilter is loaded
if ! modprobe -q nf_conntrack_ipv4; then
    modprobe nf_conntrack_ipv4

# Set up rule to ignore ICMP echo requests to broadcast addresses
if ! ip6tables -t raw -C PREROUTING -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j NOTRACK 2>/dev/null; then
    ip6tables -t raw -A PREROUTING -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j NOTRACK
fi

# Set up rule to ignore ICMP echo requests to multicast addresses
if ! ip6tables -t raw -C PREROUTING -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -d 224.0.0.0/24 -j NOTRACK 2>/dev/null; then
    ip6tables -t raw -A PREROUTING -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -d 224.0.0.0/24 -j NOTRACK
fi

# Ensure the rules persist across reboots by adding them to /etc/sysctl.conf
if ! grep -q 'net.ipv6.icmp_ignore_bcast_echos' /etc/sysctl.conf; then
    echo 'net.ipv6.icmp_ignore_bcast_echos = 1' >> /etc/sysctl.conf
fi

if ! grep -q 'net.ipv6.icmp_ignore_multicast_echos' /etc/sysctl.conf; then
    echo 'net.ipv6.icmp_ignore_multicast_echos = 1' >> /etc/sysctl.conf
fi

sysctl -p

    # Add a new GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT line
    echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"$GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT $OBJECT_POISONING\"" >> /etc/default/grub

if systemctl is-active --quiet chronyd; then
    systemctl stop chronyd
fi

# Configure chronyd to operate in client-only mode
cat << 'EOF' | tee /etc/chrony.conf.d/client-only.conf
server 0.rhel.pool.ntp.org iburst
driftfile /var/lib/chrony/drift
local stratum 10
EOF

# Reload chronyd configuration and start it
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now chronyd
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum polling interval for NTP servers
MAX_INTERVAL=6

# Get the current list of NTP servers from /etc/ntp.conf
current_servers=$(grep -oP 'server \K[^ ]+' /etc/ntp.conf)

# Loop through each server and set the maxpoll value if it doesn't exist or is less than MAX_INTERVAL
for server in $current_servers; do
    # Check if the server already has a maxpoll directive with a value greater than MAX_INTERVAL
    existing_maxpoll=$(grep -oP "maxpoll \K[0-9]+" /etc/ntp.conf | grep -w "$server")
    if [[ -z "$existing_maxpoll" || $existing_maxpoll -lt $MAX_INTERVAL ]]; then
        # Add or update the maxpoll directive for the server
        sed -i "/$server/a\ maxpoll $MAX_INTERVAL" /etc/ntp.conf
    fi
done

# Restart the ntpd service to apply changes
systemctl restart ntpd

#!/bin/bash

# Define the list of SSH-related files to check
SSH_FILES=(~/.ssh/authorized_keys ~/.ssh/config)

# Function to check file permissions and ownership
check_ssh_files() {
    for file in "${SSH_FILES[@]}"; do
        if [ -e "$file" ]; then
            # Check if the file is world-writable
            if [ -w "$file" ] && ! [ -w "$(dirname "$file")/." ]; then
                echo "File $file is world-writable, changing permissions."
                chmod go-w "$file"
            fi

            # Check if the file or directory is owned by root
            owner=$(stat -c '%U' "$file")
            if [ "$owner" = 'root' ]; then
                echo "File $file is owned by root, changing ownership to user."
                chown $(whoami) "$file"
            fi

            # Check if the directory has world-executable permissions
            dir=$(dirname "$file")
            if [ -d "$dir" ] && [ -x "$(dirname "$file")/." ]; then
                echo "Directory $dir is world-executable, changing permissions."
                chmod go-x "$dir"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Check and enforce the SSH-related files
check_ssh_files

# Create a cron job to run this script at boot time
CRON_JOB="@reboot /path/to/this/script.sh"

# Check if the cron job already exists
if ! crontab -l | grep -q "$CRON_JOB"; then
    # Add the cron job
    echo "$CRON_JOB" | crontab -
fi

        if [ -e "$file" ]; then

            if [ -w "$file" ] && ! [ -w "$(dirname "$file")/." ]; then

            if [ -d "$dir" ] && [ -x "$(dirname "$file")/." ]; then

#!/bin/bash

# Check if SSH server configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    echo "SSH server configuration file does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup original sshd_config
cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak

# Read the current SSH server configuration
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Check if the line contains 'LoginGraceTime'
    if [[ $line == *LoginGraceTime* ]]; then
        # Replace with 10 seconds to ensure user is prompted for password
        echo "LoginGraceTime 10" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    elif [[ $line == *PrintLastLogins* ]]; then
        # If already set, do nothing
        continue
    else
        # Append the line to the new sshd_config file
        echo "$line" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    fi
done < /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Enable PrintLastLogins option
echo "PrintLastLogins yes" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

# Check if SSH server configuration file exists, if not create it
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config

    echo "KeyRegenerationInterval $MAX_TIME" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

if [ -z "$current_max_time" ]; then
    echo "MaxAuthTries 3" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum amount of data and time for key renegotiation
MAX_DATA=524288000 # 512MB in bytes
MAX_TIME=3600      # 1 hour in seconds

# Check if SSH server configuration file exists, if not create it
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

# Read the current configuration and check for existing settings
current_max_data=$(grep "KeyRegenerationInterval" /etc/ssh/sshd_config | awk '{print $2}' | cut -d' ' -f1)
current_max_time=$(grep "MaxAuthTries" /etc/ssh/sshd_config | awk '{print $2}' | cut -d' ' -f1)

# If the settings are not already set, add them to the configuration file
if [ -z "$current_max_data" ]; then
    echo "KeyRegenerationInterval $MAX_TIME" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

if [ -z "$current_max_time" ]; then
    echo "MaxAuthTries 3" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd
```

# Ensure /dev/urandom is used as entropy source for SSH server
sed -i '/^UseDNS/s/^/#/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo "UseDNS no" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Generate 32 bytes of high-quality randomness and seed the SSH server's random-number generator
dd if=/dev/urandom bs=1 count=32 | tee /var/lib/ssh-agent/rand-seed

# Update SSH server configuration to use the new entropy source
echo "SeedRandom /var/lib/ssh-agent/rand-seed" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config

systemctl restart sshd

dnf install -y opensc-pkcs11

auth required pam_pkcs11.so id=smartcard
auth sufficient pam_unix.so nullok try_first_pass
EOF

systemctl edit --full --now sshd
sed -i '/^PasswordAuthentication/s/^#.*/PasswordAuthentication yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

else
    # Enable USB device authorization service to start on boot
    systemctl enable usbdeviceauth
    echo "USB device authorization service enabled to start on boot."

    # Start the USB device authorization service if not already running
    if ! systemctl is-active --quiet usbdeviceauth; then
        systemctl start usbdeviceauth
        echo "USB device authorization service started."
    else
        echo "USB device authorization service is active."
    fi
fi
```

#!/bin/bash

# Lock audit rules file to prevent modification during runtime
chmod 444 /etc/audit/audit.rules

# Create a symbolic link from the immutable version of the rules file to the location where the audit daemon reads its rules
ln -sf /etc/audit/immutable_audit_rules /etc/audit/audit.rules

# Ensure that the immutable version of the rules is used on reboot by creating a startup script
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/systemd/system/audit-restore.service
[Unit]
Description=Restore audit rules on boot

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "ln -sf /etc/audit/immutable_audit_rules /etc/audit/audit.rules"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Enable the startup script to run on reboot
systemctl enable audit-restore.service

# Define the sudo configuration directory
SUDO_CONF_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"

# Check if audit rule already exists

# If the rule does not exist, add it to the audit rules file
if [ "$AUDIT_RULE" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "-a always,exit -F path=$SUDO_CONF_DIR -F perm=write,attr=any -F arch=b64 -k sudo_modification" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
fi

# Apply the audit rules and reload the audit daemon
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
systemctl restart auditd

        auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F key="$AUDIT_RULE_ID"
    fi
else
    # Rule does not exist, create and enable it
    auditctl -w /usr/sbin/"$PRIVILEGED_COMMAND" -p x -k "$AUDIT_RULE_ID"
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F key="$AUDIT_RULE_ID"
fi

systemctl restart auditd

if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then

# Create a systemd unit file for disk-full action
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/audit-disk-full.service
[Unit]
Description=Switch to single-user mode if audit volume is full

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/sbin/telinit 1

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/audit-disk-full.timer
[Unit]
Description=Check if audit volume is full and switch to single-user mode if it is

[Timer]
OnDiskFullSec=60
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
EOF

# Load the timer unit file
systemctl daemon-reload

# Start the timer
systemctl start audit-disk-full.timer

OnDiskFullSec=60
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
EOF

# Load the timer unit file
systemctl daemon-reload

# Start the timer
systemctl start audit-disk-full.timer
```

#!/bin/bash

# Set low disk space threshold to 10%
LOW_DISK_SPACE_THRESHOLD=10

# Configure auditd to send email alerts when disk space falls below threshold
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/audit/rules.d/low-disk-space.rules
-w /var/log/audit/audit.log -p wa -k low_disk_space
EOF

# Update audit rules and ensure they persist across reboots
sudo auditctl -w /var/log/audit/audit.log -p wa -k low_disk_space
sudo systemctl restart auditd

# Set up a cron job to check disk space and send email alert if below threshold
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/cron.daily/check_low_disk_space.sh
#!/bin/bash

# Check available disk space on root partition
AVAILABLE_SPACE=$(df --output=avail / | tail -n 1)

# Calculate the low disk space threshold in percentage
THRESHOLD=$((AVAILABLE_SPACE * LOW_DISK_SPACE_THRESHOLD / 100))

# Send email alert if available disk space is below threshold
if [ "$AVAILABLE_SPACE" -lt "$THRESHOLD" ]; then
    echo "Low disk space on root partition: $AVAILABLE_SPACE available out of $(df --output=avail / | tail -n 1) bytes." | sudo mail -s "Low Disk Space Alert" admin@example.com
fi
EOF

# Make the script executable and ensure it persists across reboots
sudo chmod +x /etc/cron.daily/check_low_disk_space.sh
sudo systemctl enable cronie.service

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit log directory and threshold
AUDIT_LOG_DIR="/var/log/audit"
THRESHOLD=25

# Calculate the current free space in percentage
FREE_SPACE=$(df -h "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | tail -1 | awk '{print $4}' | sed 's/%//g')

# Check if the free space is below the threshold
if [ "$FREE_SPACE" -lt "$THRESHOLD" ]; then
    # Trigger low-space notification action (e.g., send an email)
    echo "Warning: Audit log disk space is critically low at $FREE_SPACE%." | mail -s "Audit Log Low Space Alert" admin@example.com
fi

# Set the audit system to enforce the threshold on boot
cat << EOF > /etc/systemd/system/audit-space-watch.service
[Unit]
Description=Watch for low disk space on audit logs and notify

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "/usr/local/bin/watch-audit-space.sh"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Create the watch script if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f /usr/local/bin/watch-audit-space.sh ]; then
    cat << EOF > /usr/local/bin/watch-audit-space.sh
#!/bin/bash
AUDIT_LOG_DIR="/var/log/audit"
THRESHOLD=25
FREE_SPACE=$(df -h "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | tail -1 | awk '{print $4}' | sed 's/%//g')
if [ "$FREE_SPACE" -lt "$THRESHOLD" ]; then
    echo "Warning: Audit log disk space is critically low at $FREE_SPACE%." | mail -s "Audit Log Low Space Alert" admin@example.com
fi
EOF

# Make the watch script executable
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/watch-audit-space.sh

# Enable and start the service to run on boot
systemctl enable audit-space-watch.service
systemctl start audit-space-watch.service

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure auditd is running
systemctl start auditd

# Set the hostname to FQDN if it's not already set
hostnamectl set-hostname $(hostname --fqdn)

# Configure auditd to use the FQDN as node name
sed -i '/^node_name/ s/.*/node_name = "$(hostname --fqdn)"/' /etc/audit/auditd.conf

# Ensure audit rules are updated to include node name in the log
cat <<EOF | auditctl
-w /var/log/audit/audit.log -p warx -k audit-node-name
EOF

# Reload auditd configuration
systemctl restart auditd

systemctl start auditd

# Set the hostname to FQDN if it's not already set

# Configure auditd to use the FQDN as node name
sed -i '/^node_name/ s/.*/node_name = "$(hostname --fqdn)"/' /etc/audit/auditd.conf

EOF

systemctl restart auditd

# Define the audit rule files to check
audit_rule_files=(
    /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules
)

# Loop through each file and set the correct permissions
for file in "${audit_rule_files[@]}"; do
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        chmod 0600 "$file"
    fi
done
